lines.text: Good evening. It's 19:00 Madison time, and the Purge is officially underway.
I'm Quinn Foley. You're listening to PRGE — Purge Radio — and we are live for the next twelve hours.
We're broadcasting by backup power tonight. You'll notice the station ID's running on a loop — that's not a technical failure, that's a precaution.
The grid's already showing strain. Tim's got us on three separate battery systems, and we're redundant on the antenna.
Confirmed. We took a direct hit on the main feed about twenty minutes before commencement. Transformer on Regent Street. We've rerouted through the secondary.
Signal is clean. We're running at full broadcast capacity. If you're hearing this, we're reaching you.
Tim's being modest. What he means is they tried to find us before we even went live, and he moved the whole rig by hand.
Not by hand. I had help.
The point is: we're here. We're solid. And we're going to be here all night.
Now, I know some of you are listening because you're locked in your homes and you need a voice. I know some of you are listening because you always listen, and tonight is no different.
And I know some of you are listening because you're terrified.
That's fair. I'm terrified. I'm here on a pirate broadcast one night a year because my own city would arrest me any other time, so let's not pretend this is normal.
But it is what it is. It's 19:00. The law is suspended. And we're going to get through this together.
Let's start with the weather, because weather is the thing none of us can predict and all of us need to know.
Thanks, Quinn. I'm Caroline, and I've been watching the overnight pattern all week.
Clear skies right now. Temperature holding at 41 degrees Fahrenheit. Southwest wind at 8 miles per hour.
What you need to know: this is a good night for barricading. No precipitation. No surprises from the atmosphere.
However — and this is important — a cold front is tracking in from the northwest, and it's moving faster than the models suggested three days ago.
I'm expecting the wind to pick up around 22:00 or 22:30. That's when you'll see the real shift.
By midnight, we could be looking at wind gusts over 20 miles per hour. Possibly sleet after 00:30.
The reason I'm telling you this at the top of the night: cold does two things. It keeps some people inside. And it makes the people outside move faster.
Both of those things matter. If you're secure in your home, that's good news. If you're not, that's something to think about.
Sunrise is at 6:47 tomorrow morning. That's all the math you need to know.
Holden here. Sports desk.
Quick pre-Purge statistical rundown, because people ask why I talk about this on a night like tonight.
There's a pattern. Historical data shows that neighborhoods with organized community response — watch groups, barricades, visible coordination — see lower casualty rates.
Not lower crime. Lower *casualties*. Bigger difference.

The Willy Street neighborhood watch has been active for six years. They've deployed armed perimeters on three occasions. Each time, the surrounding blocks show approximately 15% fewer deaths than the Madison average.
I'm not saying watch groups prevent the Purge. That's impossible. What I'm saying is: preparation works. Visibility works. People see organized resistance and they move to easier targets.
Last year's kill count was 187 across Madison proper. That's down from the historical 192-kill average.
Why? Cold front suppressed activity hours 2 through 6. Weather matters. Visibility matters. Preparation matters.
If you're home tonight, you've already made the best decision you can make.
Holden, you do realize you're giving a motivational speech on the first hour of a night where people are about to start dying in earnest.
I'm aware. Context matters. People need to understand that this night is not random.
It's not random. It's just legal.
Yes.
Caroline, are you noting Holden's implicit optimism for the overnight?
I'm noting it. I'm also noting that the cold front arrival time is still a variable, and if it accelerates, the window for outdoor activity shortens.
Which means, statistically, fewer hours of exposure.
Exactly.
So what you're both saying is: it could have been worse. The weather's cooperating. Preparation pays off. And we're {{ELAPSED}} into a twelve-hour night.
That's the summary. Now let's talk about what we're actually seeing on the ground.
We have field reporters positioned in four hot zones: Capitol Square, East Side, Willy Street, and the Beltline corridor.
Early reports are sparse. First hour is typically quiet. People are still settling. This is not yet the time when things go bad.
When does it go bad, Tim?
21:00 to 21:30. That's when you see the real acceleration. Nine-to-one odds.
So we've got roughly two hours of relative quiet.
Correct.

Use those two hours wisely, folks. If you haven't barricaded your doors, do it now. If you haven't checked on a family member you're sheltering, check now. If you have medication that you need to take, take it now.
After 21:00, we move into the time when PRGE becomes less about preparation and more about survival.
We'll be here the whole time. We'll give you real information when we have it. We'll keep the power on if we can.
We'll keep talking, because silence is the worst thing that happens on a night like this.
Quick technical note: if you're listening on a radio that's running on batteries, this is a good time to test the reception. Make sure you're locked in.
Our signal should be clean all night, but the power outages mean some repeater stations are offline. You may lose us briefly if you're on the far south side.
If that happens, stay tuned. Tim will reroute through backup frequencies.
We have backup frequencies. They're not advertised publicly, but if we go down, listeners will know.
One historical note about power outages on Purge Night: they actually correlate with *lower* activity in affected neighborhoods, not higher.
Darkness is disorienting. Participants tend to congregate in lit areas. If your neighborhood is dark and quiet, that's a survival advantage.
So Holden's saying: if the lights go out, that might actually be good news for you.
Statistically, yes.
I love that Holden has found a way to make a power failure sound like a blessing.
It's not a blessing. It's a context. Context is what we're trading in tonight.
Right. We're the context people. We're PRGE. We tell you what's happening so you can understand where you are in the cycle of the night.
Speaking of which: we have some early calls coming in. Text-in messages from listeners already barricaded. We'll be taking those throughout the hour.
If you're listening and you want to reach out, you can. We won't put you on air unless you ask us to. But we're here.
First message in is from someone calling themselves 'Safe on North Side.' Just says: 'Thanks for being there.'
We're here. That's the only answer to that.
Second message is from a listener in Atwood: 'Windows are boarded. Dogs are calm. How long until we know if it's going to be bad?'
The answer is: 21:00. That's your first inflection point. If you make it past midnight, you're in the fatigue hours where activity naturally drops.

Though Caroline's cold front could change that calculus. If the temperature drops faster than predicted, outdoor activity might compress into a shorter window, which paradoxically could mean higher intensity.
Holden just said that colder weather might make things *worse*. I was hoping he'd say something encouraging.
I'm not here to encourage. I'm here to give you accurate information.
Fair. Accurate information is better than comfort.
All right. Let's move into the news ticker for the top of hour one.
What we're tracking as we go into the first hour of the Purge.
First item: Capitol Square fortifications are holding. No breach attempts yet. The government's Level-10+ protection detail is operational.
For people who don't know what that means: government officials above a certain clearance level get federal protection during the Purge. It's in the law.
The people not protected are you. The people protected are the ones who made the law.
The Capitol has been breached once: 2159. Five killed in the clearing action before the inner ring held.
Since then, they've upgraded the fortifications. Current assessment: extremely unlikely to see another breach.
The wind could be a factor. Heavy wind makes it harder to approach the building undetected. Another point in the 'cold front helps people stay safe' column.
Second ticker item?
East Side neighborhoods are reporting normal early-night activity. No coordinated group movements. No organized violence yet.
That could change. East Side has accounted for roughly 18% of the city's kill count for four consecutive years.
East Side is demographically diverse, economically vulnerable, and has limited community watch organization. The data suggests it's the path of least resistance.
Which means if you live on the East Side and you're listening, you're probably nervous right now.
The answer is: stay inside. Stay locked. Stay quiet. And we'll be tracking the night as it unfolds.
Third item: Willy Street neighborhood watch is preparing their annual armed perimeter.
They've done it for six years running. Last year they held the Yahara River bridge for ninety minutes with zero casualties inside their perimeter.
This year they're expecting similar conditions. The cold front will be an advantage — harder for people to approach in poor visibility.
Willy Street's success rate is an outlier. They have trained personnel, visible coordination, and community buy-in. You cannot replicate that without six years of preparation.
But if you *are* on Willy Street, the data says your neighborhood is about 15% safer than the city average.
Fourth item: emergency services are standing by. Post-Purge triage will begin at 07:00 sharp. MorningCare has 120 personnel positioned at strategic locations.
What that means: at 07:00 when the all-clear sounds, there will be medical personnel ready to move. They will save people who would have died without them.
That's how this works. Twelve hours of anything-goes. Twelve hours of law suspended. And then we rebuild.
Some of those people will be saved because of decisions made tonight. Decisions to stay home. Decisions to barricade. Decisions to trust that we'll make it to 07:00.
All right. We're going to transition now to the deeper cuts of the first hour. Music is coming up. Wellness block. Extended commercial break for our sponsors.
But before we go there, I want to acknowledge something: it's 19:00 Madison time. It's 7 PM. It's early evening.
And the Purge is underway.
We're {{ELAPSED}} in. Eleven hours and fifty-three minutes until the all-clear.
You're listening to PRGE. We're here. And we're going to make it.
Signal strength is holding. Backup power at full capacity. We're go for the long night.

tickerItems.text: Power outages reported on Regent St, Gorman St, and corridor sections of the Beltline. Backup systems in place. Grid controller reports non-critical disruption.
Capitol Square fortifications operational. Government Level-10+ detail confirmed secure. No breach attempts in first hour.
East Side neighborhoods: normal early-night activity. No coordinated group movements yet. Historically volatile zone. Stay alert.
Willy Street neighborhood watch: preparing annual armed perimeter. Six-year track record of holding Yahara River bridge with zero interior casualties.
Madison-wide kill count as of 19:45: 2 confirmed. YoY pace on track. First-hour baseline is historically low.
MorningCare emergency triage: 120 personnel positioned. 07:00 all-clear mobilization protocol confirmed. Post-event medical response ready.
Listener from North Side: 'Windows boarded, family safe, radio is good company.' PRGE acknowledges.
PurgeShield panel installations: 48-hour emergency slots still available through dawn. Contact info in next break.
